output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.95, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.4, "R1.5": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.5, "R1.7": 0.2}}

Summary: Flagged a load-bearing internal contradiction between Section 3.1 and Section 3.3, plus framing creep at the end that undermines the stated contribution; provided concrete fixes.

**1. Hard contradiction between 3.1 and 3.3 (must fix).**
Section 3.1 states the router "consumes only the raw user query as input" and "does not invoke either downstream model at routing time" — routing in 8 ms on CPU with "no GPU activation of the local 7B prior to the routing decision." Section 3.3 then reports the strongest ablation removes "the local 7B's first-token logits from the router's input feature set," and concludes "probing the local model's response distribution is the dominant signal driving the router's decisions." Those two descriptions cannot both be true. Either:
- (a) The router does invoke the local 7B for a partial forward pass (first-token logits) — in which case the 8 ms / CPU-only / "raw query only" claims in 3.1 are wrong, and the cost accounting for the 41% savings needs to include that forward pass; or
- (b) The router uses precomputed/offline logits or a distilled proxy — in which case 3.3 needs to say so explicitly, and the ablation framing ("removing first-token logits") needs to be re-described.

This contradiction is the single most important thing to resolve before the section is reviewable. The 41% cost-reduction headline depends on which version is true.

**2. The cost claim and the quality claim are reported on different axes.**
The abstract paragraph reports "41% cost reduction at matched answer quality on MS MARCO QA." Section 3.3 reports "92% of the cloud model's nDCG@10" while "invoking the cloud model on only 38% of queries." 92% of cloud nDCG is not "matched answer quality," and "38% cloud invocations" is not a cost number (cost depends on token counts and per-token pricing, not invocation rate). Pick one operating point and report (a) cost reduction in dollars or FLOPs, and (b) the quality delta on the same axis — ideally with a Pareto curve rather than a single point.

**3. The closing paragraph undercuts the paper's own contribution.**
The section opens by claiming the central contribution is the calibrated difficulty classifier for routing, and ends by claiming the "most enduring contribution" is a general-purpose difficulty estimator with applications to index-tier selection, beam-width adaptation, and reranking depth — none of which are evaluated here. The rho=0.58 BEIR correlation is offered as the sole evidence and is described as "preliminary." Either:
- Cut the last paragraph and keep the paper focused on routing, or
- Move difficulty estimation into the contribution statement up top and add at least one downstream task evaluation. As written, the section promises two papers and delivers one.

**4. Missing details that reviewers will ask about.**
- The 24,000 training pairs: what's the source query distribution, and is it disjoint from the MS MARCO QA eval split? Label leakage from LLM-as-judge on overlapping queries would be a fatal flaw.
- "LLM-as-judge protocol" — which judge model, what prompt, what inter-judge agreement? A single sentence with a forward reference is enough.
- Temperature scaling calibration: report ECE before/after, not just that you did it.
- Table 2 is referenced but the section lists only one ablation. Either describe the full table briefly or stop calling out "the strongest ablation" as if comparing among many.
- "Acceptable response" is the training label but is never operationalized.

**5. The "query-length heuristic" comparison needs a number.**
"The router degrades to roughly the level of a query-length heuristic" is the kind of claim a reviewer will circle. Give the heuristic's nDCG and the ablated router's nDCG side by side.

**6. Prose nits.**
- "Pareto-optimal cost-quality trade-offs" in the first sentence is unsupported without a Pareto curve; soften to "favorable" unless you show the frontier.
- "Cheapest model expected to meet a user-specified quality threshold" — the router predicts local-model acceptability, not cheapest-meeting-threshold; these are equivalent only in the two-model case. Say so or generalize.
- "Routing decisions are made in 8 ms" — specify hardware (CPU model) and batch size.
- "rho=0.58 to per-query nDCG variance" is awkward; if rho is Spearman against per-query nDCG, say "Spearman rho = 0.58 with per-query nDCG."

**Suggested reordering.** Move the contribution-framing sentence after the contradiction in 3.1/3.3 is resolved. Currently the abstract paragraph, 3.1, and the closing paragraph each frame the contribution differently (cost router; lightweight classifier; difficulty estimator). Pick one and have all three reinforce it.

Uncertainty: I have not verified that DistilBERT is 110M parameters (it is typically cited as ~66M); the author should double-check this number. I have also assumed MS MARCO QA's standard eval protocol uses nDCG@10 — if a different cutoff is standard for the QA variant being used, that should be stated.
